title: Moules à la crème
subtitle: Mussels steamed in white wine and finished with cream
excerpt: Fresh mussels steamed open in white wine with shallots, garlic, and thyme, then finished with double cream and a handful of parsley. Ready in fifteen minutes from a standing start. The cooking liquid is the sauce.
sourceNotes: Moules à la crème is a Norman and Belgian variant of moules marinières, enriched with cream after the mussels steam open. Both versions are documented in classic French and Belgian cookery from the nineteenth century. The cream variant is particularly associated with the Normandy coast and Belgian brasseries.

content.text: Mussels are among the fastest things you can cook for dinner: ten minutes from a cold pan to the table, and the cooking liquid becomes the sauce without any further work. The cream goes in last, off the heat or over very low heat. It only needs to warm through, not reduce. Overcooking mussels past the moment they open makes them tough and rubbery.

Discard any mussel that does not close when tapped before cooking, and any that remain closed after two minutes of steaming. Everything else is fine. Serve with plenty of crusty bread or frites to mop up the liquid, and eat from the shell with a discarded mussel shell as a scoop.

items.name: Mussels
items.prepNote: scrubbed and debearded, any open ones discarded

content.text: Build the base and steam the mussels

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large casserole over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}}, {{garlic}}, and {{thyme}} and cook for 3 minutes without browning. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and raise the heat to high. When the wine boils, tip in all the {{mussels}}. Cover immediately and cook for 2 minutes, shaking the pan once. Lift the lid: the mussels should be open. Give any closed ones 1 further minute; discard any that remain shut.

Reduce the heat to low. Pour the {{double-cream}} of cream around the edges of the pan, stir gently to combine with the juices, and heat for 1 minute. Do not boil. Scatter the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley over, season with black pepper (no salt, the mussels provide enough), and serve immediately in the pan or in deep bowls.

items.fix: The moment the shells open wide, the mussels are done; remove immediately and serve
items.cause: Overcooked past the moment they opened

items.fix: Remove the mussels to a warm bowl, reduce the liquid briefly over high heat, then stir in the cream
items.cause: Mussels released a lot of liquid; cream was not stirred in properly

items.fix: Add the cream off the heat and stir to combine; it only needs to warm through, not cook
items.symptom: Sauce has split into cream and sea water

items.fix: Do not add salt, the mussels season the dish themselves; taste before adding any
items.cause: Salt added on top of the natural mussel brine

content.text: Moules à la crème belongs to the coastal cooking of northern France and Belgium, where mussels are farmed at scale along the Atlantic coast and the North Sea. The plainer marinières version (wine, shallots, parsley, no cream) is the reference dish, and the cream version is its richer Norman sibling. In Belgium, a kilo of mussels and a portion of frites is a national institution from September to April, served at brasseries from Brussels to the coast. The cream version is the winter order.
